---
title: "Milestones"
subtitle: "Five things you will have built by 11 September. Each one is something you'd be willing to show a colleague."
---

Days pass and leave nothing behind unless you write something down. These five are the artifacts the
bootcamp is actually for — the rest of the days feed them.

---

## Milestone 1 — A GPT you wrote yourself {#m1}

**[Day 28](days/day-28.qmd), Thursday 20 August.** Fed by days 22–27.

A decoder-only transformer you implemented, trained, and understand line by line, written up as a short
report.

**What's in it**

- The architecture, with your parameter count derived by hand and then checked against the code. If the
  two numbers disagree, the report is finding out why.
- The experiment table from day 25: context length, `n_layer`, `n_head`, `n_embd`, learning rate and
  dropout against final validation loss. Say which change mattered and which didn't.
- Samples at three sampling temperatures, and one sentence on what temperature is actually doing to the
  distribution.
- A short section on what nanoGPT does that yours doesn't, and why those things exist.

**How you know it's good** — you can hand someone your `model.py`, and answer any "why is this line here?"
question about it without looking anything up.

---

## Milestone 2 — Fine-tuned versus prompted, evaluated properly {#m2}

**[Day 35](days/day-35.qmd), Thursday 27 August.** Fed by days 29–34.

The first real report on the PubMed abstract task. The point of this one is that it is **not a single
accuracy number**, because a single accuracy number is how clinical AI results get overclaimed.

**What's in it**

- The task, the label scheme, and the train/validation/test split — fixed here and not touched again for
  the rest of the bootcamp.
- Accuracy and per-class performance for the prompting baseline and the fine-tune. Confidence intervals,
  since you know how to compute them.
- **Calibration**: a reliability curve and expected calibration error. A model that is 95% confident and
  70% correct is a different failure from one that is 70% confident and 70% correct.
- **Latency and cost** per 1000 abstracts for both approaches.
- **A failure taxonomy you built by reading actual errors** — not a confusion matrix, a set of named
  failure modes with an example of each.

**How you know it's good** — it contains at least one finding that would make you *not* deploy the better-scoring
model.

---

## Milestone 3 — The whole pipeline, traced {#m3}

**[Day 45](days/day-45.qmd), Sunday 6 September.** Fed by day 44.

nanochat is the only place you'll see every stage in one readable repo: tokenizer training, pretraining,
mid-training, SFT, RL, evaluation, and serving. The deliverable is a written trace of the path from raw
text to a model answering in a browser.

**What's in it**

- Each stage, the script that runs it, what goes in, what comes out, and roughly what it costs in GPU-hours.
- For each stage, the thing you built earlier that corresponds to it — your BPE from day 18, your training
  loop from day 24, your fine-tune from day 34.
- The two or three stages you could not have guessed were necessary before reading it.

Running the speedrun yourself is optional. Reading it properly is not.

---

## Milestone 4 — Retrieval over your own corpus {#m4}

**[Day 47](days/day-47.qmd), Tuesday 8 September.** Fed by day 46.

A working RAG system over the PubMed abstracts you've been carrying since day 18.

**What's in it**

- Chunking strategy and why you chose it. Abstracts are short — say whether chunking helped at all.
- Embeddings, an index, and top-k retrieval.
- **Retrieval quality measured separately**: recall@k and MRR against a small set of questions where you
  know which abstract holds the answer.
- **End-to-end answer quality measured separately.**
- One worked example where retrieval succeeded and generation still got it wrong, and one of the reverse.

**How you know it's good** — you can say which of your end-to-end failures are retrieval failures and which
are generation failures. Conflating the two is the single most common way RAG evaluations mislead.

---

## Milestone 5 — Capstone {#m5}

**[Day 50](days/day-50.qmd), Friday 11 September.**

Two things.

**The final report** pulls together days 33 to 49 into one evaluation of the biomedical task across every
approach you tried — zero-shot prompt, few-shot prompt, full fine-tune, LoRA, and RAG — on the same fixed
test set, with the same metrics, plus the error taxonomy. It ends with a recommendation and the conditions
under which you'd change it.

**The self-assessment** is the [self-check page](assessment.qmd), answered out loud, without notes. Anything
you can't answer is not a failure of the bootcamp; it's the reading list for October.
